methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
ParityQC is a Series A quantum computing company founded by Wolfgang Lechner and Magdalena Hauser. The valuation exercise draws from a sector universe of 11 peers: 3 public companies (IonQ, Rigetti Computing, and Quantinuum via QBTS) and 8 private firms including IonQ (private rounds), Quantinuum, D-Wave Quantum, and Infleqtion.

Standard peer-median methodology is not applicable here. The quantum computing sector lacks sufficient granularity at the Series A stage to establish a reliable narrow peer set; only 0 same-stage peers, 1 adjacent-stage peer, and 0 similar-valuation-tier peers were identified, falling short of the 3+ datapoint threshold required for statistical validity. Drawing the median from all 11 sector peers would conflate disparate stages and business models—from pre-revenue platforms to revenue-generating systems—and would introduce unwarranted noise into the valuation.

Without a sufficiently dense comparable universe at ParityQC's specific stage and valuation tier, traditional triangulation is not supported by the data. The analysis cannot responsibly propose a valuation range. The key uncertainties are: (1) whether relevant private Series A pricing data exists within the broader quantum computing market; (2) how to weight the heterogeneity of business models across the public comp set; and (3) whether adjacent sectors (e.g., quantum software, quantum simulation) offer instructive parallels. Further data collection on recent private rounds and more granular peer segmentation is required before quantitative synthesis.
